label: Newton's First Law
claim: An object stays at rest or in uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an external net force.
evidence: An object stays at rest or in uniform motion in a straight line unless acted upon by an external net force.

label: Newton's Second Law
claim: The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass.
evidence: The acceleration of an object is directly proportional to the net force acting on it and inversely proportional to its mass.

label: Newton's Third Law
claim: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.
evidence: For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.

label: Newton's First Law
claim: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.
evidence: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

evidence: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion with the same speed and in the same direction unless acted upon by an unbalanced force.

label: Definition of Force
claim: A force is a push or a pull.
evidence: A force is a push or a pull.

label: Contact Forces
claim: Contact forces are forces that act between two objects which are physically touching each other.
evidence: Contact forces are forces that act between two objects which are physically touching each other.

label: Friction
claim: Friction is a force that always opposes motion.
evidence: Friction is a force that always opposes motion.

label: Newton's First Law
claim: In the absence of unbalanced forces, an object will continue to move in a straight line at constant speed (i.e. move with constant velocity).
evidence: Newton’s first law states that, in the absence of unbalanced forces, an object will continue to move in a straight line at constant speed (i.e. move with constant velocity).

label: Newton's Second Law
claim: A resultant force will cause an object to accelerate and that the acceleration is proportional to the size of the resultant force (resultant force = mass × acceleration).
evidence: Newton’s second law states that a resultant force will cause an object to accelerate and that the acceleration is proportional to the size of the resultant force. resultant force = mass × acceleration.

label: Force Measurement
claim: Force is measured in newtons (N).
evidence: Force is measured in newtons (N).

label: Force as a vector
claim: Force is a vector quantity, meaning both its size and direction are important.
evidence: Force is a vector quantity - direction is important as well as size.

label: Newton's Third Law
claim: When forces act between two objects, both objects experience an equal size force, but in opposite directions.
evidence: When forces act between two objects, both objects experience an equal size force, but in opposite directions. This is Newton's third law of motion.

label: Friction
claim: Friction is a force that exists between two surfaces that are in contact.
evidence: Friction is a force that exists between two surfaces that are in contact.

label: Friction Example (Usefulness)
claim: The friction between our shoes and the ground helps us to walk.
evidence: The friction between our shoes and the ground helps us to walk.

label: Air Resistance
claim: Air resistance is a type of friction that bodies moving through the air experience and acts to oppose motion.
evidence: Air resistance is a type of friction which bodies that are moving through the air experience. It also acts to oppose motion.

label: Resultant Force (Same Direction)
claim: When two forces act in the same direction, the resultant force is larger than either individual force, and it is calculated by adding their sizes together.
evidence: Two forces that act in the same direction produce a resultant force that is larger than either individual force. You can easily calculate the resultant force of two forces that act in a straight line in the same direction by adding their sizes together.

label: Resultant Force (Same Direction)
claim: When two forces act in the same direction, the resultant force is the sum of the individual forces.
evidence: Two forces acting in the same direction Resultant force F = 3 N + 2 N = 5 N to the right.

label: Resultant Force (Opposite Directions)
claim: When two forces act in opposite directions, the resultant force is the difference between the magnitudes of the two forces, and its direction is the same as the larger force.
evidence: Two forces that act in opposite directions produce a resultant force that is smaller than either individual force. To find the resultant force subtract the magnitude of the smaller force from the magnitude of the larger force. The direction of the resultant force is in the same direction as the larger force.

label: Newton's First Law
claim: Newton’s First Law states that a body will remain at rest or continue to move at a constant speed in a straight line unless a resultant force acts on it.
evidence: Newton’s First Law states that a body will remain at rest or continue to move at constant speed in a straight line, unless a resultant force acts on it

label: Balanced Forces
claim: Balanced forces, acting on the same object, have no effect on the motion of that object.
evidence: Balanced forces This simply means that balanced forces, acting on the same object, will have no effect on the motion of an object.

label: Example of Balanced Forces (Stationary)
claim: If forces are balanced and an object is at rest, it remains at rest.
evidence: If it is at rest, it remains at rest.

label: Example of Balanced Forces (Constant Speed)
claim: If forces are balanced and an object is moving at a constant speed in a straight line, it continues to move at the same speed in the same straight line.
evidence: If it is moving at constant speed in a straight line, it continues to move at the same speed in the same straight line.

label: First Law of Motion (Balanced Forces)
claim: If the forces on an object are balanced (no resultant force), it stays at rest if it was at rest, or keeps moving at a constant speed in a straight line (constant velocity) if it was moving.
evidence: If the forces on an object are balanced (no resultant force) then: if it is at rest, it stays at rest; if it is moving, it keeps on moving at a constant speed in a straight line (constant velocity.)

label: Resultant Force Definition
claim: A resultant force is the single force acting on the object when all the individual forces have been combined.
evidence: A resultant force is the single force acting on the object when all the individual forces have been combined.

label: Newton's Second Law (Force and Acceleration Relationship)
claim: Resultant force (F) and acceleration (a) are directly proportional; if you double the resultant force acting on an object, you double its acceleration.
evidence: The graph is a straight line through the origin. This tells us that resultant force, F, and acceleration, a, are directly proportional. If you double the resultant force acting on an object, you double its acceleration.

label: Graphical Representation of Newton's Second Law
claim: A graph plotting resultant force (F) on the y-axis against acceleration (a) on the x-axis should be a straight line passing through the origin.
evidence: Plot a graph of resultant force F in N on the y-axis against acceleration a in m/s2 on the x-axis. Draw the line of best fit. The graph is a straight line through the origin.

label: Force and Acceleration Symbols
claim: F represents force and a represents acceleration.
evidence: F = force; a = acceleration

label: Effect of Unbalanced Forces
claim: When forces acting on an object do not balance, the resultant force causes the object to accelerate in the direction of the resultant force.
evidence: When the forces acting on an object do not balance, the resultant force causes the object to accelerate in the direction of the resultant force.

label: Acceleration and Force Relationship (Constant Mass)
claim: Acceleration is directly proportional to the resultant force if the mass remains constant.
evidence: Acceleration is directly proportional to resultant force if the mass remains constant.

label: Acceleration and Mass Relationship (Constant Force)
claim: Acceleration is inversely proportional to mass if the resultant force remains constant.
evidence: Acceleration is inversely proportional to mass if the resultant force remains constant.

label: Effect of Mass Change on Acceleration
claim: If you double the mass, you halve the acceleration (if the resultant force remains constant).
evidence: If you double the mass, you half the acceleration.

label: Result of Unbalanced Forces
claim: Unbalanced forces will cause acceleration, deceleration, or a change in direction.
evidence: This simply means that unbalanced forces will cause: acceleration; deceleration; a change in direction.

label: Newton's Second Law
claim: The relationship between resultant force (F), mass (m), and acceleration (a) is F = ma.
evidence: Resultant force F = mass m x acceleration a F = ma

label: Unit of Force
claim: The unit of force is the newton (N).
evidence: The unit of force is the newton N.

label: Newton's Second Law Interpretation
claim: One newton (1 N) is the resultant force that gives a mass of 1 kg an acceleration of 1 m/s^2 in the direction of the force.
evidence: One newton is the resultant force that gives a mass of 1 kg an acceleration of 1 m/s2 in the direction of the force. 1 N = 1 kg x 1 m/s2

label: Calculating Acceleration
claim: To calculate acceleration using F = ma, F must be the resultant force acting on the object.
evidence: When using the equation F = ma it is important to remember that F is the resultant force acting on the object.

label: Calculating Acceleration Formula
claim: Acceleration can be calculated using the formula a = F / m.
evidence: a = F / m

label: Newton's First Law
claim: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at a constant speed and in a straight line unless an unbalanced force acts on it.
evidence: An object at rest remains at rest, and an object in motion remains in motion at constant speed and in a straight line unless acted on by an unbalanced force.

label: Inertia
claim: The tendency of an object to resist changes in its state of motion is called inertia.
evidence: This tendency to resist changes in a state of motion is inertia.

label: Newton's Second Law
claim: The acceleration of an object depends on the mass of the object and the amount of force applied.
evidence: The acceleration of an object depends on the mass of the object and the amount of force applied.

label: Newton's Third Law
claim: Whenever one object exerts a force on another object, the second object exerts an equal and opposite force on the first.
evidence: Whenever one object exerts a force on another object, the second object exerts an equal and opposite on the first.

label: Application of Laws
claim: Newton's laws explain the relationship between a physical object and the forces acting upon it, providing the basis of modern physics.
evidence: Sir Isaac Newton’s laws of motion explain the relationship between a physical object and the forces acting upon it. Understanding this information provides us with the basis of modern physics.

label: Newton's Second Law (Momentum Definition)
claim: A force is defined as the change in momentum (mass times velocity) per change in time.
evidence: His second law defines a force to be equal to change in momentum (mass times velocity) per change in time.

label: Momentum Definition
claim: Momentum is defined as the mass of an object times its velocity (m V).
evidence: Momentum is defined to be the mass m of an object times its velocity V.

label: Second Law for Constant Mass
claim: For an object with a constant mass (m), Newton's second law can be written as Force equals mass times acceleration (F = m a).
evidence: The second law then reduces to the more familiar product of a mass and an acceleration: F = m * a

label: Second Law for Changing Mass
claim: When mass is not constant (like a bottle rocket), you can only look at changes in momentum, not separate changes in mass and velocity.
evidence: Newton’s second law talks about changes in momentum (m V). So, at this point, we can’t separate out how much the mass changed and how much the velocity changed.

label: Second Law for Constant Mass (Velocity Form)
claim: If mass is constant, the force applied is equal to the mass multiplied by the change in velocity divided by the change in time (F = m * (V1 – V0) / (t1 – t0)).
evidence: For a constant mass m, Newton’s second law looks like: F = m * (V1 – V0) / (t1 – t0)

label: First Law of Motion (Inertia)
claim: An object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion stays in motion at a constant speed and in a straight line unless an unbalanced force acts on it.
evidence: An object at rest remains at rest, and an object in motion remains in motion at constant speed and in a straight line unless acted on by an unbalanced force.

label: Relationship between Force and Acceleration
claim: An object subjected to an external force will accelerate, and the amount of acceleration is proportional to the size of the force and inversely proportional to the mass of the object.
evidence: an object subjected to an external force will accelerate and that the amount of the acceleration is proportional to the size of the force. The amount of acceleration is also inversely proportional to the mass of the object; for equal forces, a heavier object will experience less acceleration than a lighter object.

label: Momentum and Force
claim: A force causes a change in velocity, and a change in velocity generates a force; these concepts are linked.
evidence: a force causes a change in velocity; and likewise, a change in velocity generates a force. The equation works both ways.

label: Newton’s Third Law (Action-Reaction)
claim: For every action (force) in nature, there is an equal and opposite reaction; if object A pushes object B, object B pushes object A with an equal and opposite force.
evidence: for every action (force) in nature there is an equal and opposite reaction. If object A exerts a force on object B, object B also exerts an equal and opposite force on object A.

label: Vector Quantity
claim: Velocity, force, acceleration, and momentum all have both a magnitude and a direction associated with them; these are called vector quantities.
evidence: The velocity, force, acceleration, and momentum have both a magnitude and a direction associated with them. Scientists and mathematicians call this a vector quantity.

label: Newton's Second Law of Motion
claim: The acceleration of an object depends on the mass of the object and the amount of force applied.
evidence: The acceleration of an object depends on the mass of the object and the amount of force applied.

label: Newton's Third Law of Motion
claim: Whenever one object exerts a force on another object, the second object exerts an equal and opposite force on the first.
evidence: Whenever one object exerts a force on another object, the second object exerts an equal and opposite force on the first.
